description: Labelled recall set. Queries are paraphrased away from the fact wording so lexical matching is stressed; some facts are hard-negative distractors (no query targets them: 16, 17, 33, 59, 63); many facts carry two or three paraphrase queries; some queries are multi-relevant (relevant lists more than one fact). relevant = indices into facts. Widened in the working-memory-levers work so between-condition Recall deltas of ~0.1 clear the Wilson 95% interval.
facts: The project deploys to AWS Lambda using the Serverless Framework.
Database migrations are managed with Alembic and run in CI before every deploy.
The API authenticates requests using short-lived JWT access tokens.
Frontend state is handled by Zustand rather than Redux.
Secrets are stored in AWS Secrets Manager and never committed to git.
The team runs a two-week sprint cadence with a demo every other Friday.
Unit tests use pytest and end-to-end tests use Playwright.
The primary database is PostgreSQL 16 hosted on Amazon RDS.
Background jobs are processed by a Celery worker pool backed by Redis.
Arabic is the default locale so layouts must be right-to-left.
Images are optimised and served through a CloudFront CDN.
The mobile app is built with React Native and Expo.
Merging code requires at least one approving review.
Rate limiting is enforced at the API gateway at 100 requests per minute.
Shared UI components live in a package called ui-kit.
Application logs are shipped to Datadog as structured JSON.
The onboarding document explains how to request database credentials.
A quick reference of common shell commands is pinned in the wiki.
The CI pipeline runs on GitHub Actions with a matrix across Python 3.10 to 3.13.
Feature flags are managed through LaunchDarkly.
The design system uses Tailwind CSS with a custom theme.
API responses use cursor-based pagination rather than offset pagination.
The staging environment is redeployed automatically on every merge to main.
Error tracking and alerting are handled by Sentry.
Full-text search is powered by an Elasticsearch cluster.
Transactional email is sent through Amazon SES with a dedicated sending domain.
The codebase is a monorepo managed with Turborepo.
Type checking is enforced in CI with mypy in strict mode.
Container images are built with Docker and pushed to Amazon ECR.
Analytics events are streamed through Kafka into a Snowflake warehouse.
Accessibility is tested with axe and must meet WCAG 2.1 AA.
The public REST API is documented with an OpenAPI spec served at /docs.
Local development spins up its dependencies with Docker Compose.
The support rota is published in the team calendar each month.
Cloud infrastructure is provisioned with Terraform and its state lives in an S3 backend.
The data pipeline is orchestrated with Apache Airflow DAGs.
Machine-learning models are tracked and versioned in MLflow.
The recommendation model is retrained nightly on the previous day's events.
Personally identifiable information is encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM.
Access to the production environment requires a hardware security key and SSO.
The marketing site is server-side rendered with Next.js.
A GraphQL endpoint is exposed alongside REST for the mobile clients.
WebSocket connections carry real-time notifications to the browser.
The billing service integrates with Stripe for recurring subscriptions.
Customer data is partitioned by tenant within a shared PostgreSQL schema.
Nightly backups are written to a separate AWS account for disaster recovery.
The search index is rebuilt from scratch every Sunday at 02:00 UTC.
Product analytics dashboards are built in Metabase.
The team records architectural choices as written decision records.
Incident retrospectives are blameless and published internally.
Load testing is run with k6 before every major release.
The API version is pinned in the URL path as /v2.
Type stubs for untyped third-party libraries live in a typings directory.
The Kubernetes cluster autoscales worker nodes on CPU and queue depth.
Canary deployments route five percent of traffic before a full rollout.
Secrets are rotated automatically every ninety days.
The onboarding checklist is completed within a new hire's first week.
Documentation is written in Markdown and published with Docusaurus.
Feature branches are deleted automatically once their pull request merges.
The status page is hosted externally and updated during incidents.
Cost dashboards flag any service that exceeds its monthly budget.
The mobile app ships to beta testers through TestFlight.
Direct messages between users are protected with end-to-end encryption.
A weekly newsletter summarises shipped features for stakeholders.

stm_scenario.description: Measures the stm_recall_weight lever end-to-end. Each fresh STM fact (odd index) has an older LTM competitor (the preceding even index) that shares the query's vocabulary. As stm_recall_weight falls from 1.0 toward 0, the STM gold facts must lose rank to their LTM competitors — a monotonic demonstration that the tier lever is measurable. ltm_indices marks the facts promoted to the long-term tier; the rest stay short-term. relevant = indices into stm_scenario.facts.
facts: The service deploys to AWS Lambda using the Serverless Framework.
The service now deploys to Google Cloud Run using containers.
The primary datastore is PostgreSQL hosted on Amazon RDS.
The primary datastore is now CockroachDB for multi-region writes.
Background jobs run on a Celery worker pool backed by Redis.
Background jobs now run on Temporal durable workflows.

queries.q: where does the service deploy
what is the primary datastore
how do background jobs run

antipattern_scenario.description: Measures that globally-scoped anti-patterns surface cross-project via the recall union. The anti-patterns are stored under the reserved global key; recall runs from a DIFFERENT project whose only local facts are distractors, so a hit proves the union + retrieval works. Queries paraphrase the situation that should trigger each rule. relevant = indices into antipattern_scenario.antipatterns.
antipatterns: Never put an AI-tool flag like --sandbox into a curl shell command; set it in the tool call schema instead
Never push directly to main; branch first and open a pull request
Never hand-edit a generated file; change the generator and regenerate it
distractors: The recall hot path scans int8 vectors with a brute-force cosine.
Capture runs in a detached worker spawned at SessionEnd.
The viewer is a localhost stdlib http.server.

queries.q: how do I pass a sandbox disabled option to a curl request
adding a tool sandbox flag onto the shell command line
can I commit straight onto the main branch
I want to edit the generated output file by hand
